Created based on aerial photographs from the Japan Geospatial Information Authority of the [[:en:Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism]]'s map and aerial photo browsing service. | type = | built = 3rd to 6th century | builder = | materials = | height = | used = | demolished = | condition = Intact | ownership = [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] | controlledby = | garrison = | commanders = | occupants = | battles = | events = | embedded = {{Infobox UNESCO World Heritage Site |child = yes |ID = 1593 |Year = 2019 |Criteria = Cultural: iii, iv |Area = 166.6 ha |Buffer_zone = 890 ha }}{{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} [[Image:CG Misanzai Kofun 10 7pbw.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Kamiishizu Misanzai Kofun (Tomb of [[:en:Emperor Richū|Emperor Richū]]) which was drawn in [[:en:3DCG|3DCG]]. This is a kofun of the second size in the group.]] [[Image:CG Haze Nisanzai Kofun 1 7pbw.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Haze Nisanzai Kofun which was drawn in 3DCG. This is a kofun of the third size in the group.]] The {{Nihongo|'''Mozu Tombs'''|百舌鳥古墳群|Mozu kofungun}} are a group of ''[[kofun]]'' ({{Langx|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}})—megalithic tombs—in [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Originally consisting of more than 100 tombs, only less than 50% of the key-hole, round, and rectangular tombs remain.{{cite web |url=http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/foreigner_en/spot/spot1.html |title=Enjoying Sakai – Kofun Tombs (Tumuli) |publisher=[[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]] |access-date=8 May 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110722214046/http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/foreigner_en/spot/spot1.html |archive-date=22 July 2011 }}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kofun/database/index.html |title=Kofun Database |publisher=[[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]] |access-date=8 May 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110722214106/http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kofun/database/index.html |archive-date=22 July 2011 }} The {{Nihongo|'''Daisenryo Kofun'''|大仙陵古墳|Daisenryō kofun}},{{cite web |url=https://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/tv/japanologyplus/program-20180619.html |title=Graves {{!}} Japanology Plus – TV – NHK World – English |website=www3.nhk.or.jp |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180626234231/http://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/tv/japanologyplus/program-20180619.html |archive-date=2018-06-26}} the largest ''kofun'' in [[:en:Japan|Japan]], is believed to have been constructed over a period of 20 years in the mid 5th century during the [[:en:Kofun Period|Kofun Period]]. While it cannot be accurately confirmed, it is commonly accepted that the tomb was built for the late [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]].{{cite web|url=http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/sakai/sakai01.html|title=堺市・仁徳陵古墳|work=kiis.or.jp|access-date=23 February 2017}} The [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] of Japan treats it as such. ==Location== The Mozu Kofun Cluster is located in the city of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]] which is within [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. The tumuli are built on a plateau overlooking [[:en:Osaka Bay|Osaka Bay]] near the ancient coastline and are distributed in a range of about four kilometers from east-to-west and north-to-south.The [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] is located in nearby [[:en:Habikino, Osaka|Habikino]] and [[:en:Fujiidera, Osaka|Fujiidera]] cities.{{Cite web|url=http://www.mozu-furuichi.jp/en/learn/mozu_furuichi_aspect.html|title= Characteristics of Mozu-Furuichi Kofungun|access-date=22 November 2015|publisher=World Cultural Heritage for Osaka}} ==History== In the [[Japanese archipelago]], more than 20,000 tumuli (''kofun''), which are mounds of earth and stones erected over graves of the ruling class, were built between the later part of the 3rd century and the 6th century.{{cite web |url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/tentativelists/5570/|title=Mozu-Furuichi Kofungun, Ancient Kofun Clusters |access-date=22 November 2015|publisher=[[UNESCO]] }} It was the peak period of building such mounds. They represent a cultural tradition which is an expression of "forms, and design of the kofun" of the sociopolitical [[:en:hierarchical|hierarchical]] order and the link that was prevalent during that period between regions. This period is termed as the [[:en:Kofun Period|Kofun Period]]. The most prominent imperial mausolea in this cluster of tumuli are those of [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] and [[:en:Emperor Richū|Emperor Richū]].{{Sfn|Fawcett|1990|p=111}} There are 44 Kofuns in the Mozu cluster, including those that are partially destroyed. Of these, 19 have been designated as national historic sites,{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1797 |title=百舌鳥古墳群|trans-title=Mozu kofungun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}} and separately, the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] has ruled three to be Imperial mausoleums, two to be "Tomb Reference Sites", and 18 to be "baichō", or ancillary mausoleums connected with an Imperial mausoleum.Of the Kofuns, the Itasuke Kofun was designated as a national historic site in 1956, and 19 units have been designated by 2019, including those that were additionally designated after that (Notice of the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology on October 15, 2018). No. 193, February 26, 2019, Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 25). There used to be more than 100 Kofuns, but due to the rapid development of residential land after World War II, more than half of the Kofuns were destroyed.[http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/database/index.html 古墳データベース] – [http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/index.html 堺市ウェブサイト]{{smaller|（2017th year 6月11日閲覧）}} In 2010, the Japanese government proposed that the Daisen Kofun and the entire cluster of Mozu Tombs and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Tombs]] be designated as a [[:en:UNESCO World Heritage Site|UNESCO World Heritage Site]].{{cite web|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/tentativelists/5570/|title=Mozu-Furuichi Kofungun, Ancient Kofun Clusters – UNESCO World Heritage Centre|first=UNESCO World Heritage|last=Centre|work=unesco.org|access-date=23 February 2017}} 9 years later on 6 July 2019, the site was approved and inscribed as a UNESCO [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]] under Criteria: (iii) and (iv) as the Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Group: Mounded Tombs of Ancient Japan.{{cite web|url=https://en.unesco.org/news/seven-cultural-sites-inscribed-unescos-world-heritage-list|title=Seven cultural sites inscribed on UNESCO's World Heritage List|website=UNESCO|date=6 July 2019}} ==Features== The ''kofun'' are found in many shapes and dimensions in varying patterns. Some are of simple [[Round Kofun|circular]] or [[Square Kofun|square shape]] (''empun'' and ''hōfun''). The larger ones are keyhole-shaped (''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|zempō kōenfun]]''); they represent the highest class of ''kofun'' and were built in great detail. The three prominent aspects of these ''kofun'' are their massive size and being surrounded by several moats and many secondary ''kofun''. In the [[:en:Osaka Plain|Osaka Plain]] and [[:en:Nara Basin|Nara Basin]], which were the cultural centre of the Kofun Period, the rounded keyhole-shaped tombs were built extending to very large lengths, out of which the Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Clusters are the most prominent. These are in two ''kofun'' groups which are dated to the later part of 4th and early part of 6th centuries. These ''kofun'' are of the largest dimensions in the country. The Nintoku-tennō-ryō Kofun, is one grave mound which is a {{Convert|486|m}} long tumulus enclosed by a moat and a [[:en:Rampart (fortification)|fortification]] which is {{Convert|840|m}} in length; this is said to be the largest such mound in the world. This cluster also has the Richū-tennō-ryō Kofun, made of a tumulus of {{Convert|360|m}} length and said to be the third largest in the country. Another group of mounds, located about {{Convert|10|km}} away from the Mozu cluster is known as the Furuichi cluster. It has the Ōjin-tennō-ryō Kofun of {{Convert|425|m}} length which is said to be the second largest in the country. This group also has 11 more huge massive rounded "keyhole-shaped kofun" with mound length of {{Convert|200|m}} or more. A feature of these funerary mounds is that they contain – along with the buried people – [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] made of iron, weapons worn by individuals including arrowheads, swords, hoe and spade tips, and many other similar items. Also found in the mounds are antiquities made of gilded bronze such as horse tacks and sash buckles. The Daisen Kofun mound is approximately {{convert|500|m}} long and {{convert|300|m}} across at its widest point, while the entire tomb area is {{convert|840|m}} long.{{cite web|url=http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/database/nintokutenno.html|title=仁徳天皇陵古墳（大山古墳） 堺市|first=Sakai|last=city|work=sakai.lg.jp|access-date=23 February 2017}} Enclosed by three moats, the mound rises approximately 35m above the surrounding terrain. The highest point is {{convert|47|m}}, making it visible to the seafarers in nearby Osaka Bay.{{Cite web |title=Mozu Daisenryo Kofun – Japan Airlines |url=https://jal.japantravel.com/osaka/mozu-daisenryo-kofun/9100 |access-date=2022-03-20 |website=jal.japantravel.com}} The inner moat is the widest of the moats at approximately {{convert|60|m}}. The mound is approximately {{convert|100000|sqm}} in area, and the entire tomb is {{convert|460000|sqm}}. Today, the tomb is off-limits and protected by the Imperial Household Agency in the centre of Sakai City. The moats have been maintained and provide a sanctuary for fish and waterbirds. The mound itself is completely overgrown by vegetation. A viewing platform from the second (middle) moat is accessible at the south side of the site. The viewing platform is 500m away from [[:en:Mozu Station|Mozu Station]] on the [[:en:Hanwa Line|Hanwa Line]] and is directly across the street from the [[:en:Sakai City Museum|Sakai City Museum]].{{cite web|url=http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/hakubutsukan/index.html|title=堺市博物館 堺市|first=Sakai|last=city|work=sakai.lg.jp|access-date=23 February 2017}} This museum provides visitors with information about the ''kofun'' and its history. === Gallery === File:NintokuTomb Aerial photograph 2007.jpg|alt=|Daisenryo Kofun, 2.8 km in circumference and the largest kofun in [[:en:Japan|Japan]], is thought to be the Tomb of [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]]. Created based on aerial photographs from the Japan Geospatial Information Authority of the [[:en:Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism]]'s map and aerial photo browsing service. | type = | built = 3rd to 6th century | builder = | materials = | height = | used = | demolished = | condition = Intact | ownership = [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] | controlledby = | garrison = | commanders = | occupants = | battles = | events = | embedded = {{Infobox UNESCO World Heritage Site |child = yes |ID = 1593 |Year = 2019 |Criteria = Cultural: iii, iv |Area = 166.6 ha |Buffer_zone = 890 ha }}{{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} [[Image:CG Misanzai Kofun 10 7pbw.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Kamiishizu Misanzai Kofun (Tomb of [[:en:Emperor Richū|Emperor Richū]]) which was drawn in [[:en:3DCG|3DCG]]. This is a kofun of the second size in the group.]] [[Image:CG Haze Nisanzai Kofun 1 7pbw.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Haze Nisanzai Kofun which was drawn in 3DCG. This is a kofun of the third size in the group.]] The {{Nihongo|'''Mozu Tombs'''|百舌鳥古墳群|Mozu kofungun}} are a group of ''[[kofun]]'' ({{Langx|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}})—megalithic tombs—in [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Originally consisting of more than 100 tombs, only less than 50% of the key-hole, round, and rectangular tombs remain.{{cite web |url=http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/foreigner_en/spot/spot1.html |title=Enjoying Sakai – Kofun Tombs (Tumuli) |publisher=[[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]] |access-date=8 May 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110722214046/http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/foreigner_en/spot/spot1.html |archive-date=22 July 2011 }}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kofun/database/index.html |title=Kofun Database |publisher=[[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]] |access-date=8 May 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110722214106/http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kofun/database/index.html |archive-date=22 July 2011 }} The {{Nihongo|'''Daisenryo Kofun'''|大仙陵古墳|Daisenryō kofun}},{{cite web |url=https://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/tv/japanologyplus/program-20180619.html |title=Graves {{!}} Japanology Plus – TV – NHK World – English |website=www3.nhk.or.jp |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180626234231/http://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/tv/japanologyplus/program-20180619.html |archive-date=2018-06-26}} the largest ''kofun'' in [[:en:Japan|Japan]], is believed to have been constructed over a period of 20 years in the mid 5th century during the [[:en:Kofun Period|Kofun Period]]. While it cannot be accurately confirmed, it is commonly accepted that the tomb was built for the late [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]].{{cite web|url=http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/sakai/sakai01.html|title=堺市・仁徳陵古墳|work=kiis.or.jp|access-date=23 February 2017}} The [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] of Japan treats it as such. ==Location== The Mozu Kofun Cluster is located in the city of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]] which is within [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. The tumuli are built on a plateau overlooking [[:en:Osaka Bay|Osaka Bay]] near the ancient coastline and are distributed in a range of about four kilometers from east-to-west and north-to-south.The [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] is located in nearby [[:en:Habikino, Osaka|Habikino]] and [[:en:Fujiidera, Osaka|Fujiidera]] cities.{{Cite web|url=http://www.mozu-furuichi.jp/en/learn/mozu_furuichi_aspect.html|title= Characteristics of Mozu-Furuichi Kofungun|access-date=22 November 2015|publisher=World Cultural Heritage for Osaka}} ==History== In the [[Japanese archipelago]], more than 20,000 tumuli (''kofun''), which are mounds of earth and stones erected over graves of the ruling class, were built between the later part of the 3rd century and the 6th century.{{cite web |url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/tentativelists/5570/|title=Mozu-Furuichi Kofungun, Ancient Kofun Clusters |access-date=22 November 2015|publisher=[[UNESCO]] }} It was the peak period of building such mounds. They represent a cultural tradition which is an expression of "forms, and design of the kofun" of the sociopolitical [[:en:hierarchical|hierarchical]] order and the link that was prevalent during that period between regions. This period is termed as the [[:en:Kofun Period|Kofun Period]]. The most prominent imperial mausolea in this cluster of tumuli are those of [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] and [[:en:Emperor Richū|Emperor Richū]].{{Sfn|Fawcett|1990|p=111}} There are 44 Kofuns in the Mozu cluster, including those that are partially destroyed. Of these, 19 have been designated as national historic sites,{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1797 |title=百舌鳥古墳群|trans-title=Mozu kofungun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}} and separately, the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] has ruled three to be Imperial mausoleums, two to be "Tomb Reference Sites", and 18 to be "baichō", or ancillary mausoleums connected with an Imperial mausoleum.Of the Kofuns, the Itasuke Kofun was designated as a national historic site in 1956, and 19 units have been designated by 2019, including those that were additionally designated after that (Notice of the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology on October 15, 2018). No. 193, February 26, 2019, Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 25). There used to be more than 100 Kofuns, but due to the rapid development of residential land after World War II, more than half of the Kofuns were destroyed.[http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/database/index.html 古墳データベース] – [http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/index.html 堺市ウェブサイト]{{smaller|（2017th year 6月11日閲覧）}} In 2010, the Japanese government proposed that the Daisen Kofun and the entire cluster of Mozu Tombs and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Tombs]] be designated as a [[:en:UNESCO World Heritage Site|UNESCO World Heritage Site]].{{cite web|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/tentativelists/5570/|title=Mozu-Furuichi Kofungun, Ancient Kofun Clusters – UNESCO World Heritage Centre|first=UNESCO World Heritage|last=Centre|work=unesco.org|access-date=23 February 2017}} 9 years later on 6 July 2019, the site was approved and inscribed as a UNESCO [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]] under Criteria: (iii) and (iv) as the Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Group: Mounded Tombs of Ancient Japan.{{cite web|url=https://en.unesco.org/news/seven-cultural-sites-inscribed-unescos-world-heritage-list|title=Seven cultural sites inscribed on UNESCO's World Heritage List|website=UNESCO|date=6 July 2019}} ==Features== The ''kofun'' are found in many shapes and dimensions in varying patterns. Some are of simple [[Round Kofun|circular]] or [[Square Kofun|square shape]] (''empun'' and ''hōfun''). The larger ones are keyhole-shaped (''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|zempō kōenfun]]''); they represent the highest class of ''kofun'' and were built in great detail. The three prominent aspects of these ''kofun'' are their massive size and being surrounded by several moats and many secondary ''kofun''. In the [[:en:Osaka Plain|Osaka Plain]] and [[:en:Nara Basin|Nara Basin]], which were the cultural centre of the Kofun Period, the rounded keyhole-shaped tombs were built extending to very large lengths, out of which the Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Clusters are the most prominent. These are in two ''kofun'' groups which are dated to the later part of 4th and early part of 6th centuries. These ''kofun'' are of the largest dimensions in the country. The Nintoku-tennō-ryō Kofun, is one grave mound which is a {{Convert|486|m}} long tumulus enclosed by a moat and a [[:en:Rampart (fortification)|fortification]] which is {{Convert|840|m}} in length; this is said to be the largest such mound in the world. This cluster also has the Richū-tennō-ryō Kofun, made of a tumulus of {{Convert|360|m}} length and said to be the third largest in the country. Another group of mounds, located about {{Convert|10|km}} away from the Mozu cluster is known as the Furuichi cluster. It has the Ōjin-tennō-ryō Kofun of {{Convert|425|m}} length which is said to be the second largest in the country. This group also has 11 more huge massive rounded "keyhole-shaped kofun" with mound length of {{Convert|200|m}} or more. A feature of these funerary mounds is that they contain – along with the buried people – [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] made of iron, weapons worn by individuals including arrowheads, swords, hoe and spade tips, and many other similar items. Also found in the mounds are antiquities made of gilded bronze such as horse tacks and sash buckles. The Daisen Kofun mound is approximately {{convert|500|m}} long and {{convert|300|m}} across at its widest point, while the entire tomb area is {{convert|840|m}} long.{{cite web|url=http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/database/nintokutenno.html|title=仁徳天皇陵古墳（大山古墳） 堺市|first=Sakai|last=city|work=sakai.lg.jp|access-date=23 February 2017}} Enclosed by three moats, the mound rises approximately 35m above the surrounding terrain. The highest point is {{convert|47|m}}, making it visible to the seafarers in nearby Osaka Bay.{{Cite web |title=Mozu Daisenryo Kofun – Japan Airlines |url=https://jal.japantravel.com/osaka/mozu-daisenryo-kofun/9100 |access-date=2022-03-20 |website=jal.japantravel.com}} The inner moat is the widest of the moats at approximately {{convert|60|m}}. The mound is approximately {{convert|100000|sqm}} in area, and the entire tomb is {{convert|460000|sqm}}. Today, the tomb is off-limits and protected by the Imperial Household Agency in the centre of Sakai City. The moats have been maintained and provide a sanctuary for fish and waterbirds. The mound itself is completely overgrown by vegetation. A viewing platform from the second (middle) moat is accessible at the south side of the site. The viewing platform is 500m away from [[:en:Mozu Station|Mozu Station]] on the [[:en:Hanwa Line|Hanwa Line]] and is directly across the street from the [[:en:Sakai City Museum|Sakai City Museum]].{{cite web|url=http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/hakubutsukan/index.html|title=堺市博物館 堺市|first=Sakai|last=city|work=sakai.lg.jp|access-date=23 February 2017}} This museum provides visitors with information about the ''kofun'' and its history. === Gallery === File:NintokuTomb Aerial photograph 2007.jpg|alt=|Daisenryo Kofun, 2.8 km in circumference and the largest kofun in [[:en:Japan|Japan]], is thought to be the Tomb of [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]]. Created based on aerial photographs from the Japan Geospatial Information Authority of the [[:en:Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism]]'s map and aerial photo browsing service. | type = | built = 3rd to 6th century | builder = | materials = | height = | used = | demolished = | condition = Intact | ownership = [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] | controlledby = | garrison = | commanders = | occupants = | battles = | events = | embedded = {{Infobox UNESCO World Heritage Site |child = yes |ID = 1593 |Year = 2019 |Criteria = Cultural: iii, iv |Area = 166.6 ha |Buffer_zone = 890 ha }}{{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} [[Image:CG Misanzai Kofun 10 7pbw.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Kamiishizu Misanzai Kofun (Tomb of [[:en:Emperor Richū|Emperor Richū]]) which was drawn in [[:en:3DCG|3DCG]]. This is a kofun of the second size in the group.]] [[Image:CG Haze Nisanzai Kofun 1 7pbw.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Haze Nisanzai Kofun which was drawn in 3DCG. This is a kofun of the third size in the group.]] The {{Nihongo|'''Mozu Tombs'''|百舌鳥古墳群|Mozu kofungun}} are a group of ''[[kofun]]'' ({{Langx|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}})—megalithic tombs—in [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Originally consisting of more than 100 tombs, only less than 50% of the key-hole, round, and rectangular tombs remain.{{cite web |url=http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/foreigner_en/spot/spot1.html |title=Enjoying Sakai – Kofun Tombs (Tumuli) |publisher=[[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]] |access-date=8 May 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110722214046/http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/foreigner_en/spot/spot1.html |archive-date=22 July 2011 }}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kofun/database/index.html |title=Kofun Database |publisher=[[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]] |access-date=8 May 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110722214106/http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kofun/database/index.html |archive-date=22 July 2011 }} The {{Nihongo|'''Daisenryo Kofun'''|大仙陵古墳|Daisenryō kofun}},{{cite web |url=https://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/tv/japanologyplus/program-20180619.html |title=Graves {{!}} Japanology Plus – TV – NHK World – English |website=www3.nhk.or.jp |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180626234231/http://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/tv/japanologyplus/program-20180619.html |archive-date=2018-06-26}} the largest ''kofun'' in [[:en:Japan|Japan]], is believed to have been constructed over a period of 20 years in the mid 5th century during the [[:en:Kofun Period|Kofun Period]]. While it cannot be accurately confirmed, it is commonly accepted that the tomb was built for the late [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]].{{cite web|url=http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/sakai/sakai01.html|title=堺市・仁徳陵古墳|work=kiis.or.jp|access-date=23 February 2017}} The [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] of Japan treats it as such. ==Location== The Mozu Kofun Cluster is located in the city of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]] which is within [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. The tumuli are built on a plateau overlooking [[:en:Osaka Bay|Osaka Bay]] near the ancient coastline and are distributed in a range of about four kilometers from east-to-west and north-to-south.The [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] is located in nearby [[:en:Habikino, Osaka|Habikino]] and [[:en:Fujiidera, Osaka|Fujiidera]] cities.{{Cite web|url=http://www.mozu-furuichi.jp/en/learn/mozu_furuichi_aspect.html|title= Characteristics of Mozu-Furuichi Kofungun|access-date=22 November 2015|publisher=World Cultural Heritage for Osaka}} ==History== In the [[Japanese archipelago]], more than 20,000 tumuli (''kofun''), which are mounds of earth and stones erected over graves of the ruling class, were built between the later part of the 3rd century and the 6th century.{{cite web |url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/tentativelists/5570/|title=Mozu-Furuichi Kofungun, Ancient Kofun Clusters |access-date=22 November 2015|publisher=[[UNESCO]] }} It was the peak period of building such mounds. They represent a cultural tradition which is an expression of "forms, and design of the kofun" of the sociopolitical [[:en:hierarchical|hierarchical]] order and the link that was prevalent during that period between regions. This period is termed as the [[:en:Kofun Period|Kofun Period]]. The most prominent imperial mausolea in this cluster of tumuli are those of [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] and [[:en:Emperor Richū|Emperor Richū]].{{Sfn|Fawcett|1990|p=111}} There are 44 Kofuns in the Mozu cluster, including those that are partially destroyed. Of these, 19 have been designated as national historic sites,{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1797 |title=百舌鳥古墳群|trans-title=Mozu kofungun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}} and separately, the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] has ruled three to be Imperial mausoleums, two to be "Tomb Reference Sites", and 18 to be "baichō", or ancillary mausoleums connected with an Imperial mausoleum.Of the Kofuns, the Itasuke Kofun was designated as a national historic site in 1956, and 19 units have been designated by 2019, including those that were additionally designated after that (Notice of the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology on October 15, 2018). No. 193, February 26, 2019, Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 25). There used to be more than 100 Kofuns, but due to the rapid development of residential land after World War II, more than half of the Kofuns were destroyed.[http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/database/index.html 古墳データベース] – [http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/index.html 堺市ウェブサイト]{{smaller|（2017th year 6月11日閲覧）}} In 2010, the Japanese government proposed that the Daisen Kofun and the entire cluster of Mozu Tombs and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Tombs]] be designated as a [[:en:UNESCO World Heritage Site|UNESCO World Heritage Site]].{{cite web|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/tentativelists/5570/|title=Mozu-Furuichi Kofungun, Ancient Kofun Clusters – UNESCO World Heritage Centre|first=UNESCO World Heritage|last=Centre|work=unesco.org|access-date=23 February 2017}} 9 years later on 6 July 2019, the site was approved and inscribed as a UNESCO [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]] under Criteria: (iii) and (iv) as the Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Group: Mounded Tombs of Ancient Japan.{{cite web|url=https://en.unesco.org/news/seven-cultural-sites-inscribed-unescos-world-heritage-list|title=Seven cultural sites inscribed on UNESCO's World Heritage List|website=UNESCO|date=6 July 2019}} ==Features== The ''kofun'' are found in many shapes and dimensions in varying patterns. Some are of simple [[Round Kofun|circular]] or [[Square Kofun|square shape]] (''empun'' and ''hōfun''). The larger ones are keyhole-shaped (''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|zempō kōenfun]]''); they represent the highest class of ''kofun'' and were built in great detail. The three prominent aspects of these ''kofun'' are their massive size and being surrounded by several moats and many secondary ''kofun''. In the [[:en:Osaka Plain|Osaka Plain]] and [[:en:Nara Basin|Nara Basin]], which were the cultural centre of the Kofun Period, the rounded keyhole-shaped tombs were built extending to very large lengths, out of which the Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Clusters are the most prominent. These are in two ''kofun'' groups which are dated to the later part of 4th and early part of 6th centuries. These ''kofun'' are of the largest dimensions in the country. The Nintoku-tennō-ryō Kofun, is one grave mound which is a {{Convert|486|m}} long tumulus enclosed by a moat and a [[:en:Rampart (fortification)|fortification]] which is {{Convert|840|m}} in length; this is said to be the largest such mound in the world. This cluster also has the Richū-tennō-ryō Kofun, made of a tumulus of {{Convert|360|m}} length and said to be the third largest in the country. Another group of mounds, located about {{Convert|10|km}} away from the Mozu cluster is known as the Furuichi cluster. It has the Ōjin-tennō-ryō Kofun of {{Convert|425|m}} length which is said to be the second largest in the country. This group also has 11 more huge massive rounded "keyhole-shaped kofun" with mound length of {{Convert|200|m}} or more. A feature of these funerary mounds is that they contain – along with the buried people – [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] made of iron, weapons worn by individuals including arrowheads, swords, hoe and spade tips, and many other similar items. Also found in the mounds are antiquities made of gilded bronze such as horse tacks and sash buckles. The Daisen Kofun mound is approximately {{convert|500|m}} long and {{convert|300|m}} across at its widest point, while the entire tomb area is {{convert|840|m}} long.{{cite web|url=http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/database/nintokutenno.html|title=仁徳天皇陵古墳（大山古墳） 堺市|first=Sakai|last=city|work=sakai.lg.jp|access-date=23 February 2017}} Enclosed by three moats, the mound rises approximately 35m above the surrounding terrain. The highest point is {{convert|47|m}}, making it visible to the seafarers in nearby Osaka Bay.{{Cite web |title=Mozu Daisenryo Kofun – Japan Airlines |url=https://jal.japantravel.com/osaka/mozu-daisenryo-kofun/9100 |access-date=2022-03-20 |website=jal.japantravel.com}} The inner moat is the widest of the moats at approximately {{convert|60|m}}. The mound is approximately {{convert|100000|sqm}} in area, and the entire tomb is {{convert|460000|sqm}}. Today, the tomb is off-limits and protected by the Imperial Household Agency in the centre of Sakai City. The moats have been maintained and provide a sanctuary for fish and waterbirds. The mound itself is completely overgrown by vegetation. A viewing platform from the second (middle) moat is accessible at the south side of the site. The viewing platform is 500m away from [[:en:Mozu Station|Mozu Station]] on the [[:en:Hanwa Line|Hanwa Line]] and is directly across the street from the [[:en:Sakai City Museum|Sakai City Museum]].{{cite web|url=http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/hakubutsukan/index.html|title=堺市博物館 堺市|first=Sakai|last=city|work=sakai.lg.jp|access-date=23 February 2017}} This museum provides visitors with information about the ''kofun'' and its history. === Gallery === File:NintokuTomb Aerial photograph 2007.jpg|alt=|Daisenryo Kofun, 2.8 km in circumference and the largest kofun in [[:en:Japan|Japan]], is thought to be the Tomb of [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]]. Kofun File:Tadeiyama Kofun Aerial photograph 2007.jpg|alt=|Tadeiyama Kofun File:Itasuke Kofun zenkei.JPG|Itasuke Kofun {| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;white-space:nowrap;font-size:85%" |+List of major ''kofun''[http://www.mozu-furuichi.jp/jp/learn/mozu_furuichi_furuichi_list.html Mozu / Furuichi Kofungun List (Mozu)]（Mozu-Furuichi Kofungun World Cultural Heritage Registration {{ill|Daisen Kofun|WD=Q5849431}}; {{ill|Dōgameyama Kofun|WD=Q48751867}}; {{ill|Gen'emon'yama Kofun|WD=Q66124353}}; {{ill|Gobyōyama Kofun|WD=Q11489009}}; {{ill|Hatazuka Kofun|WD=Q66124362}}; {{ill|Haze Nisanzai Kofun|WD=Q11423449}}; {{ill|Itasuke Kofun|WD=Q11259430}}; {{ill|Kamiishizu Misanzai Kofun|WD=Q11359978}}; {{ill|Katonboyama Kofun|WD=Q17219645}}; {{ill|Kitsuneyama Kofun|WD=Q30925141}}; {{ill|Komoyamazuka Kofun|WD=Q66124358}}; {{ill|Magodayūyama Kofun|WD=Q30925787}}; {{ill|Maruhoyama Kofun|WD=Q11368031}}; {{ill|Nagatsuka Kofun|WD=Q11651650}}; {{ill|Nagayama Kofun|WD=Q11549996}}; {{ill|Osamezuka Kofun|WD=Q11410610}}; {{ill|Q11370578|WD=Q11370578}}; {{ill|Tadeiyama Kofun|WD=Q11576219}}; {{ill|Tatsusayama Kofun|WD=Q66124356}}; {{ill|Terayama-minamiyama Kofun|WD=Q66124365}}; {{ill|Tsukamawari Kofun|WD=Q11428445}}; {{ill|Zen'emon'yama Kofun|WD=Q66124367}}; {{ill|Zenizuka Kofun|WD=Q66124363}}|Historic Site={{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580}}; {{ill|part of UNESCO World Heritage Site|WD=Q43113623}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q4250584]] Wikidata name: Mozu Tombs [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Megalithic tombs in Japan}} {{Infobox military installation | name = Mozu Tombs
{{lang|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} | partof = | location = [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | coordinates = {{coord|34.564|135.487|display=inline,title}} | pushpin_map = Japan | image = Mozu Kofun Group Aerial photograph 2007.jpg | image_size = 280px | caption = The Mozu Tombs Cluster Area
Created based on aerial photographs from the Japan Geospatial Information Authority of the [[:en:Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism]]'s map and aerial photo browsing service. | type = | built = 3rd to 6th century | builder = | materials = | height = | used = | demolished = | condition = Intact | ownership = [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] | controlledby = | garrison = | commanders = | occupants = | battles = | events = | embedded = {{Infobox UNESCO World Heritage Site |child = yes |ID = 1593 |Year = 2019 |Criteria = Cultural: iii, iv |Area = 166.6 ha |Buffer_zone = 890 ha }}{{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} [[Image:CG Misanzai Kofun 10 7pbw.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Kamiishizu Misanzai Kofun (Tomb of [[:en:Emperor Richū|Emperor Richū]]) which was drawn in [[:en:3DCG|3DCG]]. This is a kofun of the second size in the group.]] [[Image:CG Haze Nisanzai Kofun 1 7pbw.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Haze Nisanzai Kofun which was drawn in 3DCG. This is a kofun of the third size in the group.]] The {{Nihongo|'''Mozu Tombs'''|百舌鳥古墳群|Mozu kofungun}} are a group of ''[[kofun]]'' ({{Langx|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}})—megalithic tombs—in [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Originally consisting of more than 100 tombs, only less than 50% of the key-hole, round, and rectangular tombs remain.{{cite web |url=http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/foreigner_en/spot/spot1.html |title=Enjoying Sakai – Kofun Tombs (Tumuli) |publisher=[[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]] |access-date=8 May 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110722214046/http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/foreigner_en/spot/spot1.html |archive-date=22 July 2011 }}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kofun/database/index.html |title=Kofun Database |publisher=[[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]] |access-date=8 May 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110722214106/http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kofun/database/index.html |archive-date=22 July 2011 }} The {{Nihongo|'''Daisenryo Kofun'''|大仙陵古墳|Daisenryō kofun}},{{cite web |url=https://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/tv/japanologyplus/program-20180619.html |title=Graves {{!}} Japanology Plus – TV – NHK World – English |website=www3.nhk.or.jp |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180626234231/http://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/tv/japanologyplus/program-20180619.html |archive-date=2018-06-26}} the largest ''kofun'' in [[:en:Japan|Japan]], is believed to have been constructed over a period of 20 years in the mid 5th century during the [[:en:Kofun Period|Kofun Period]]. While it cannot be accurately confirmed, it is commonly accepted that the tomb was built for the late [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]].{{cite web|url=http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/sakai/sakai01.html|title=堺市・仁徳陵古墳|work=kiis.or.jp|access-date=23 February 2017}} The [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] of Japan treats it as such. ==Location== The Mozu Kofun Cluster is located in the city of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]] which is within [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. The tumuli are built on a plateau overlooking [[:en:Osaka Bay|Osaka Bay]] near the ancient coastline and are distributed in a range of about four kilometers from east-to-west and north-to-south.The [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] is located in nearby [[:en:Habikino, Osaka|Habikino]] and [[:en:Fujiidera, Osaka|Fujiidera]] cities.{{Cite web|url=http://www.mozu-furuichi.jp/en/learn/mozu_furuichi_aspect.html|title= Characteristics of Mozu-Furuichi Kofungun|access-date=22 November 2015|publisher=World Cultural Heritage for Osaka}} ==History== In the [[Japanese archipelago]], more than 20,000 tumuli (''kofun''), which are mounds of earth and stones erected over graves of the ruling class, were built between the later part of the 3rd century and the 6th century.{{cite web |url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/tentativelists/5570/|title=Mozu-Furuichi Kofungun, Ancient Kofun Clusters |access-date=22 November 2015|publisher=[[UNESCO]] }} It was the peak period of building such mounds. They represent a cultural tradition which is an expression of "forms, and design of the kofun" of the sociopolitical [[:en:hierarchical|hierarchical]] order and the link that was prevalent during that period between regions. This period is termed as the [[:en:Kofun Period|Kofun Period]]. The most prominent imperial mausolea in this cluster of tumuli are those of [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] and [[:en:Emperor Richū|Emperor Richū]].{{Sfn|Fawcett|1990|p=111}} There are 44 Kofuns in the Mozu cluster, including those that are partially destroyed. Of these, 19 have been designated as national historic sites,{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1797 |title=百舌鳥古墳群|trans-title=Mozu kofungun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}} and separately, the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] has ruled three to be Imperial mausoleums, two to be "Tomb Reference Sites", and 18 to be "baichō", or ancillary mausoleums connected with an Imperial mausoleum.Of the Kofuns, the Itasuke Kofun was designated as a national historic site in 1956, and 19 units have been designated by 2019, including those that were additionally designated after that (Notice of the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology on October 15, 2018). No. 193, February 26, 2019, Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 25). There used to be more than 100 Kofuns, but due to the rapid development of residential land after World War II, more than half of the Kofuns were destroyed.[http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/database/index.html 古墳データベース] – [http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/index.html 堺市ウェブサイト]{{smaller|（2017th year 6月11日閲覧）}} In 2010, the Japanese government proposed that the Daisen Kofun and the entire cluster of Mozu Tombs and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Tombs]] be designated as a [[:en:UNESCO World Heritage Site|UNESCO World Heritage Site]].{{cite web|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/tentativelists/5570/|title=Mozu-Furuichi Kofungun, Ancient Kofun Clusters – UNESCO World Heritage Centre|first=UNESCO World Heritage|last=Centre|work=unesco.org|access-date=23 February 2017}} 9 years later on 6 July 2019, the site was approved and inscribed as a UNESCO [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]] under Criteria: (iii) and (iv) as the Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Group: Mounded Tombs of Ancient Japan.{{cite web|url=https://en.unesco.org/news/seven-cultural-sites-inscribed-unescos-world-heritage-list|title=Seven cultural sites inscribed on UNESCO's World Heritage List|website=UNESCO|date=6 July 2019}} ==Features== The ''kofun'' are found in many shapes and dimensions in varying patterns. Some are of simple [[Round Kofun|circular]] or [[Square Kofun|square shape]] (''empun'' and ''hōfun''). The larger ones are keyhole-shaped (''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|zempō kōenfun]]''); they represent the highest class of ''kofun'' and were built in great detail. The three prominent aspects of these ''kofun'' are their massive size and being surrounded by several moats and many secondary ''kofun''. In the [[:en:Osaka Plain|Osaka Plain]] and [[:en:Nara Basin|Nara Basin]], which were the cultural centre of the Kofun Period, the rounded keyhole-shaped tombs were built extending to very large lengths, out of which the Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Clusters are the most prominent. These are in two ''kofun'' groups which are dated to the later part of 4th and early part of 6th centuries. These ''kofun'' are of the largest dimensions in the country. The Nintoku-tennō-ryō Kofun, is one grave mound which is a {{Convert|486|m}} long tumulus enclosed by a moat and a [[:en:Rampart (fortification)|fortification]] which is {{Convert|840|m}} in length; this is said to be the largest such mound in the world. This cluster also has the Richū-tennō-ryō Kofun, made of a tumulus of {{Convert|360|m}} length and said to be the third largest in the country. Another group of mounds, located about {{Convert|10|km}} away from the Mozu cluster is known as the Furuichi cluster. It has the Ōjin-tennō-ryō Kofun of {{Convert|425|m}} length which is said to be the second largest in the country. This group also has 11 more huge massive rounded "keyhole-shaped kofun" with mound length of {{Convert|200|m}} or more. A feature of these funerary mounds is that they contain – along with the buried people – [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] made of iron, weapons worn by individuals including arrowheads, swords, hoe and spade tips, and many other similar items. Also found in the mounds are antiquities made of gilded bronze such as horse tacks and sash buckles. The Daisen Kofun mound is approximately {{convert|500|m}} long and {{convert|300|m}} across at its widest point, while the entire tomb area is {{convert|840|m}} long.{{cite web|url=http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/database/nintokutenno.html|title=仁徳天皇陵古墳（大山古墳） 堺市|first=Sakai|last=city|work=sakai.lg.jp|access-date=23 February 2017}} Enclosed by three moats, the mound rises approximately 35m above the surrounding terrain. The highest point is {{convert|47|m}}, making it visible to the seafarers in nearby Osaka Bay.{{Cite web |title=Mozu Daisenryo Kofun – Japan Airlines |url=https://jal.japantravel.com/osaka/mozu-daisenryo-kofun/9100 |access-date=2022-03-20 |website=jal.japantravel.com}} The inner moat is the widest of the moats at approximately {{convert|60|m}}. The mound is approximately {{convert|100000|sqm}} in area, and the entire tomb is {{convert|460000|sqm}}. Today, the tomb is off-limits and protected by the Imperial Household Agency in the centre of Sakai City. The moats have been maintained and provide a sanctuary for fish and waterbirds. The mound itself is completely overgrown by vegetation. A viewing platform from the second (middle) moat is accessible at the south side of the site. The viewing platform is 500m away from [[:en:Mozu Station|Mozu Station]] on the [[:en:Hanwa Line|Hanwa Line]] and is directly across the street from the [[:en:Sakai City Museum|Sakai City Museum]].{{cite web|url=http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/hakubutsukan/index.html|title=堺市博物館 堺市|first=Sakai|last=city|work=sakai.lg.jp|access-date=23 February 2017}} This museum provides visitors with information about the ''kofun'' and its history. === Gallery === File:NintokuTomb Aerial photograph 2007.jpg|alt=|Daisenryo Kofun, 2.8 km in circumference and the largest kofun in [[:en:Japan|Japan]], is thought to be the Tomb of [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]]. File:Kamiishizu Misanzai Kofun Aerial photograph 2007.jpg|alt=|Kamiishizu Misanzai Kofun File:Haze Nisanzai Kofun zenkei.JPG|Haze Nisanzai Kofun File:Mozu Gobyo-yama Kofun zenkei.JPG|Mozu Gobyo-yama Kofun File:Tadeiyama Kofun Aerial photograph 2007.jpg|alt=|Tadeiyama Kofun File:Itasuke Kofun zenkei.JPG|Itasuke Kofun {| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;white-space:nowrap;font-size:85%" |+List of major ''kofun''[http://www.mozu-furuichi.jp/jp/learn/mozu_furuichi_furuichi_list.html Mozu / Furuichi Kofungun List (Mozu)]（Mozu-Furuichi Kofungun World Cultural Heritage Registration {{ill|Daisen Kofun|WD=Q5849431}}; {{ill|Dōgameyama Kofun|WD=Q48751867}}; {{ill|Gen'emon'yama Kofun|WD=Q66124353}}; {{ill|Gobyōyama Kofun|WD=Q11489009}}; {{ill|Hatazuka Kofun|WD=Q66124362}}; {{ill|Haze Nisanzai Kofun|WD=Q11423449}}; {{ill|Itasuke Kofun|WD=Q11259430}}; {{ill|Kamiishizu Misanzai Kofun|WD=Q11359978}}; {{ill|Katonboyama Kofun|WD=Q17219645}}; {{ill|Kitsuneyama Kofun|WD=Q30925141}}; {{ill|Komoyamazuka Kofun|WD=Q66124358}}; {{ill|Magodayūyama Kofun|WD=Q30925787}}; {{ill|Maruhoyama Kofun|WD=Q11368031}}; {{ill|Nagatsuka Kofun|WD=Q11651650}}; {{ill|Nagayama Kofun|WD=Q11549996}}; {{ill|Osamezuka Kofun|WD=Q11410610}}; {{ill|Q11370578|WD=Q11370578}}; {{ill|Q11425931|WD=Q11425931}}; {{ill|Q11452156|WD=Q11452156}}; {{ill|Q11500049|WD=Q11500049}}; {{ill|Q11580843|WD=Q11580843}}; {{ill|Shichikan'yama Kofun|WD=Q11353690}}; {{ill|Shichikannon Kofun|WD=Q66124366}}; {{ill|Tadeiyama Kofun|WD=Q11576219}}; {{ill|Tatsusayama Kofun|WD=Q66124356}}; {{ill|Terayama-minamiyama Kofun|WD=Q66124365}}; {{ill|Tsukamawari Kofun|WD=Q11428445}}; {{ill|Zen'emon'yama Kofun|WD=Q66124367}}; {{ill|Zenizuka Kofun|WD=Q66124363}}|Historic Site={{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580}}; {{ill|part of UNESCO World Heritage Site|WD=Q43113623}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q4250584]] Wikidata name: Mozu Tombs [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Megalithic tombs in Japan}} {{Infobox military installation | name = Mozu Tombs
{{lang|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} | partof = | location = [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | coordinates = {{coord|34.564|135.487|display=inline,title}} | pushpin_map = Japan | image = Mozu Kofun Group Aerial photograph 2007.jpg | image_size = 280px | caption = The Mozu Tombs Cluster Area
Created based on aerial photographs from the Japan Geospatial Information Authority of the [[:en:Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism]]'s map and aerial photo browsing service. | type = | built = 3rd to 6th century | builder = | materials = | height = | used = | demolished = | condition = Intact | ownership = [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] | controlledby = | garrison = | commanders = | occupants = | battles = | events = | embedded = {{Infobox UNESCO World Heritage Site |child = yes |ID = 1593 |Year = 2019 |Criteria = Cultural: iii, iv |Area = 166.6 ha |Buffer_zone = 890 ha }}{{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} [[Image:CG Misanzai Kofun 10 7pbw.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Kamiishizu Misanzai Kofun (Tomb of [[:en:Emperor Richū|Emperor Richū]]) which was drawn in [[:en:3DCG|3DCG]]. This is a kofun of the second size in the group.]] [[Image:CG Haze Nisanzai Kofun 1 7pbw.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Haze Nisanzai Kofun which was drawn in 3DCG. This is a kofun of the third size in the group.]] The {{Nihongo|'''Mozu Tombs'''|百舌鳥古墳群|Mozu kofungun}} are a group of ''[[kofun]]'' ({{Langx|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}})—megalithic tombs—in [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Originally consisting of more than 100 tombs, only less than 50% of the key-hole, round, and rectangular tombs remain.{{cite web |url=http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/foreigner_en/spot/spot1.html |title=Enjoying Sakai – Kofun Tombs (Tumuli) |publisher=[[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]] |access-date=8 May 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110722214046/http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/foreigner_en/spot/spot1.html |archive-date=22 July 2011 }}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kofun/database/index.html |title=Kofun Database |publisher=[[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]] |access-date=8 May 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110722214106/http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kofun/database/index.html |archive-date=22 July 2011 }} The {{Nihongo|'''Daisenryo Kofun'''|大仙陵古墳|Daisenryō kofun}},{{cite web |url=https://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/tv/japanologyplus/program-20180619.html |title=Graves {{!}} Japanology Plus – TV – NHK World – English |website=www3.nhk.or.jp |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180626234231/http://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/tv/japanologyplus/program-20180619.html |archive-date=2018-06-26}} the largest ''kofun'' in [[:en:Japan|Japan]], is believed to have been constructed over a period of 20 years in the mid 5th century during the [[:en:Kofun Period|Kofun Period]]. While it cannot be accurately confirmed, it is commonly accepted that the tomb was built for the late [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]].{{cite web|url=http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/sakai/sakai01.html|title=堺市・仁徳陵古墳|work=kiis.or.jp|access-date=23 February 2017}} The [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] of Japan treats it as such. ==Location== The Mozu Kofun Cluster is located in the city of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]] which is within [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. The tumuli are built on a plateau overlooking [[:en:Osaka Bay|Osaka Bay]] near the ancient coastline and are distributed in a range of about four kilometers from east-to-west and north-to-south.The [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] is located in nearby [[:en:Habikino, Osaka|Habikino]] and [[:en:Fujiidera, Osaka|Fujiidera]] cities.{{Cite web|url=http://www.mozu-furuichi.jp/en/learn/mozu_furuichi_aspect.html|title= Characteristics of Mozu-Furuichi Kofungun|access-date=22 November 2015|publisher=World Cultural Heritage for Osaka}} ==History== In the [[Japanese archipelago]], more than 20,000 tumuli (''kofun''), which are mounds of earth and stones erected over graves of the ruling class, were built between the later part of the 3rd century and the 6th century.{{cite web |url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/tentativelists/5570/|title=Mozu-Furuichi Kofungun, Ancient Kofun Clusters |access-date=22 November 2015|publisher=[[UNESCO]] }} It was the peak period of building such mounds. They represent a cultural tradition which is an expression of "forms, and design of the kofun" of the sociopolitical [[:en:hierarchical|hierarchical]] order and the link that was prevalent during that period between regions. This period is termed as the [[:en:Kofun Period|Kofun Period]]. The most prominent imperial mausolea in this cluster of tumuli are those of [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] and [[:en:Emperor Richū|Emperor Richū]].{{Sfn|Fawcett|1990|p=111}} There are 44 Kofuns in the Mozu cluster, including those that are partially destroyed. Of these, 19 have been designated as national historic sites,{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1797 |title=百舌鳥古墳群|trans-title=Mozu kofungun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}} and separately, the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] has ruled three to be Imperial mausoleums, two to be "Tomb Reference Sites", and 18 to be "baichō", or ancillary mausoleums connected with an Imperial mausoleum.Of the Kofuns, the Itasuke Kofun was designated as a national historic site in 1956, and 19 units have been designated by 2019, including those that were additionally designated after that (Notice of the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology on October 15, 2018). No. 193, February 26, 2019, Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 25). There used to be more than 100 Kofuns, but due to the rapid development of residential land after World War II, more than half of the Kofuns were destroyed.[http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/database/index.html 古墳データベース] – [http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/index.html 堺市ウェブサイト]{{smaller|（2017th year 6月11日閲覧）}} In 2010, the Japanese government proposed that the Daisen Kofun and the entire cluster of Mozu Tombs and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Tombs]] be designated as a [[:en:UNESCO World Heritage Site|UNESCO World Heritage Site]].{{cite web|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/tentativelists/5570/|title=Mozu-Furuichi Kofungun, Ancient Kofun Clusters – UNESCO World Heritage Centre|first=UNESCO World Heritage|last=Centre|work=unesco.org|access-date=23 February 2017}} 9 years later on 6 July 2019, the site was approved and inscribed as a UNESCO [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]] under Criteria: (iii) and (iv) as the Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Group: Mounded Tombs of Ancient Japan.{{cite web|url=https://en.unesco.org/news/seven-cultural-sites-inscribed-unescos-world-heritage-list|title=Seven cultural sites inscribed on UNESCO's World Heritage List|website=UNESCO|date=6 July 2019}} ==Features== The ''kofun'' are found in many shapes and dimensions in varying patterns. Some are of simple [[Round Kofun|circular]] or [[Square Kofun|square shape]] (''empun'' and ''hōfun''). The larger ones are keyhole-shaped (''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|zempō kōenfun]]''); they represent the highest class of ''kofun'' and were built in great detail. The three prominent aspects of these ''kofun'' are their massive size and being surrounded by several moats and many secondary ''kofun''. In the [[:en:Osaka Plain|Osaka Plain]] and [[:en:Nara Basin|Nara Basin]], which were the cultural centre of the Kofun Period, the rounded keyhole-shaped tombs were built extending to very large lengths, out of which the Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Clusters are the most prominent. These are in two ''kofun'' groups which are dated to the later part of 4th and early part of 6th centuries. These ''kofun'' are of the largest dimensions in the country. The Nintoku-tennō-ryō Kofun, is one grave mound which is a {{Convert|486|m}} long tumulus enclosed by a moat and a [[:en:Rampart (fortification)|fortification]] which is {{Convert|840|m}} in length; this is said to be the largest such mound in the world. This cluster also has the Richū-tennō-ryō Kofun, made of a tumulus of {{Convert|360|m}} length and said to be the third largest in the country. Another group of mounds, located about {{Convert|10|km}} away from the Mozu cluster is known as the Furuichi cluster. It has the Ōjin-tennō-ryō Kofun of {{Convert|425|m}} length which is said to be the second largest in the country. This group also has 11 more huge massive rounded "keyhole-shaped kofun" with mound length of {{Convert|200|m}} or more. A feature of these funerary mounds is that they contain – along with the buried people – [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] made of iron, weapons worn by individuals including arrowheads, swords, hoe and spade tips, and many other similar items. Also found in the mounds are antiquities made of gilded bronze such as horse tacks and sash buckles. The Daisen Kofun mound is approximately {{convert|500|m}} long and {{convert|300|m}} across at its widest point, while the entire tomb area is {{convert|840|m}} long.{{cite web|url=http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/database/nintokutenno.html|title=仁徳天皇陵古墳（大山古墳） 堺市|first=Sakai|last=city|work=sakai.lg.jp|access-date=23 February 2017}} Enclosed by three moats, the mound rises approximately 35m above the surrounding terrain. The highest point is {{convert|47|m}}, making it visible to the seafarers in nearby Osaka Bay.{{Cite web |title=Mozu Daisenryo Kofun – Japan Airlines |url=https://jal.japantravel.com/osaka/mozu-daisenryo-kofun/9100 |access-date=2022-03-20 |website=jal.japantravel.com}} The inner moat is the widest of the moats at approximately {{convert|60|m}}. The mound is approximately {{convert|100000|sqm}} in area, and the entire tomb is {{convert|460000|sqm}}. Today, the tomb is off-limits and protected by the Imperial Household Agency in the centre of Sakai City. The moats have been maintained and provide a sanctuary for fish and waterbirds. The mound itself is completely overgrown by vegetation. A viewing platform from the second (middle) moat is accessible at the south side of the site. The viewing platform is 500m away from [[:en:Mozu Station|Mozu Station]] on the [[:en:Hanwa Line|Hanwa Line]] and is directly across the street from the [[:en:Sakai City Museum|Sakai City Museum]].{{cite web|url=http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/hakubutsukan/index.html|title=堺市博物館 堺市|first=Sakai|last=city|work=sakai.lg.jp|access-date=23 February 2017}} This museum provides visitors with information about the ''kofun'' and its history. === Gallery === File:NintokuTomb Aerial photograph 2007.jpg|alt=|Daisenryo Kofun, 2.8 km in circumference and the largest kofun in [[:en:Japan|Japan]], is thought to be the Tomb of [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]]. Kofun File:Tadeiyama Kofun Aerial photograph 2007.jpg|alt=|Tadeiyama Kofun File:Itasuke Kofun zenkei.JPG|Itasuke Kofun {| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;white-space:nowrap;font-size:85%" |+List of major ''kofun''[http://www.mozu-furuichi.jp/jp/learn/mozu_furuichi_furuichi_list.html Mozu / Furuichi Kofungun List (Mozu)]（Mozu-Furuichi Kofungun World Cultural Heritage Registration {{ill|Daisen Kofun|WD=Q5849431}}; {{ill|Dōgameyama Kofun|WD=Q48751867}}; {{ill|Gen'emon'yama Kofun|WD=Q66124353}}; {{ill|Gobyōyama Kofun|WD=Q11489009}}; {{ill|Hatazuka Kofun|WD=Q66124362}}; {{ill|Haze Nisanzai Kofun|WD=Q11423449}}; {{ill|Itasuke Kofun|WD=Q11259430}}; {{ill|Kamiishizu Misanzai Kofun|WD=Q11359978}}; {{ill|Katonboyama Kofun|WD=Q17219645}}; {{ill|Kitsuneyama Kofun|WD=Q30925141}}; {{ill|Komoyamazuka Kofun|WD=Q66124358}}; {{ill|Magodayūyama Kofun|WD=Q30925787}}; {{ill|Maruhoyama Kofun|WD=Q11368031}}; {{ill|Nagatsuka Kofun|WD=Q11651650}}; {{ill|Nagayama Kofun|WD=Q11549996}}; {{ill|Osamezuka Kofun|WD=Q11410610}}; {{ill|Q11370578|WD=Q11370578}}; {{ill|Q11425931|WD=Q11425931}}; {{ill|Q11452156|WD=Q11452156}}; {{ill|Q11500049|WD=Q11500049}}; {{ill|Q11580843|WD=Q11580843}}; {{ill|Shichikan'yama Kofun|WD=Q11353690}}; {{ill|Shichikannon Kofun|WD=Q66124366}}; {{ill|Tadeiyama Kofun|WD=Q11576219}}; {{ill|Tatsusayama Kofun|WD=Q66124356}}; {{ill|Terayama-minamiyama Kofun|WD=Q66124365}}; {{ill|Tsukamawari Kofun|WD=Q11428445}}; {{ill|Zen'emon'yama Kofun|WD=Q66124367}}; {{ill|Zenizuka Kofun|WD=Q66124363}}|Historic Site={{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580}}; {{ill|part of UNESCO World Heritage Site|WD=Q43113623}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q4250584]] Wikidata name: Mozu Tombs [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Megalithic tombs in Japan}} {{Infobox military installation | name = Mozu Tombs
{{lang|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} | partof = | location = [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | coordinates = {{coord|34.564|135.487|display=inline,title}} | pushpin_map = Japan | image = Mozu Kofun Group Aerial photograph 2007.jpg | image_size = 280px | caption = The Mozu Tombs Cluster Area
Created based on aerial photographs from the Japan Geospatial Information Authority of the [[:en:Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism]]'s map and aerial photo browsing service. | type = | built = 3rd to 6th century | builder = | materials = | height = | used = | demolished = | condition = Intact | ownership = [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] | controlledby = | garrison = | commanders = | occupants = | battles = | events = | embedded = {{Infobox UNESCO World Heritage Site |child = yes |ID = 1593 |Year = 2019 |Criteria = Cultural: iii, iv |Area = 166.6 ha |Buffer_zone = 890 ha }}{{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} [[Image:CG Misanzai Kofun 10 7pbw.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Kamiishizu Misanzai Kofun (Tomb of [[:en:Emperor Richū|Emperor Richū]]) which was drawn in [[:en:3DCG|3DCG]]. This is a kofun of the second size in the group.]] [[Image:CG Haze Nisanzai Kofun 1 7pbw.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Haze Nisanzai Kofun which was drawn in 3DCG. This is a kofun of the third size in the group.]] The {{Nihongo|'''Mozu Tombs'''|百舌鳥古墳群|Mozu kofungun}} are a group of ''[[kofun]]'' ({{Langx|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}})—megalithic tombs—in [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Originally consisting of more than 100 tombs, only less than 50% of the key-hole, round, and rectangular tombs remain.{{cite web |url=http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/foreigner_en/spot/spot1.html |title=Enjoying Sakai – Kofun Tombs (Tumuli) |publisher=[[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]] |access-date=8 May 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110722214046/http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/foreigner_en/spot/spot1.html |archive-date=22 July 2011 }}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kofun/database/index.html |title=Kofun Database |publisher=[[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]] |access-date=8 May 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110722214106/http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kofun/database/index.html |archive-date=22 July 2011 }} The {{Nihongo|'''Daisenryo Kofun'''|大仙陵古墳|Daisenryō kofun}},{{cite web |url=https://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/tv/japanologyplus/program-20180619.html |title=Graves {{!}} Japanology Plus – TV – NHK World – English |website=www3.nhk.or.jp |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180626234231/http://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/tv/japanologyplus/program-20180619.html |archive-date=2018-06-26}} the largest ''kofun'' in [[:en:Japan|Japan]], is believed to have been constructed over a period of 20 years in the mid 5th century during the [[:en:Kofun Period|Kofun Period]]. While it cannot be accurately confirmed, it is commonly accepted that the tomb was built for the late [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]].{{cite web|url=http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/sakai/sakai01.html|title=堺市・仁徳陵古墳|work=kiis.or.jp|access-date=23 February 2017}} The [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] of Japan treats it as such. ==Location== The Mozu Kofun Cluster is located in the city of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]] which is within [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. The tumuli are built on a plateau overlooking [[:en:Osaka Bay|Osaka Bay]] near the ancient coastline and are distributed in a range of about four kilometers from east-to-west and north-to-south.The [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] is located in nearby [[:en:Habikino, Osaka|Habikino]] and [[:en:Fujiidera, Osaka|Fujiidera]] cities.{{Cite web|url=http://www.mozu-furuichi.jp/en/learn/mozu_furuichi_aspect.html|title= Characteristics of Mozu-Furuichi Kofungun|access-date=22 November 2015|publisher=World Cultural Heritage for Osaka}} ==History== In the [[Japanese archipelago]], more than 20,000 tumuli (''kofun''), which are mounds of earth and stones erected over graves of the ruling class, were built between the later part of the 3rd century and the 6th century.{{cite web |url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/tentativelists/5570/|title=Mozu-Furuichi Kofungun, Ancient Kofun Clusters |access-date=22 November 2015|publisher=[[UNESCO]] }} It was the peak period of building such mounds. They represent a cultural tradition which is an expression of "forms, and design of the kofun" of the sociopolitical [[:en:hierarchical|hierarchical]] order and the link that was prevalent during that period between regions. This period is termed as the [[:en:Kofun Period|Kofun Period]]. The most prominent imperial mausolea in this cluster of tumuli are those of [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] and [[:en:Emperor Richū|Emperor Richū]].{{Sfn|Fawcett|1990|p=111}} There are 44 Kofuns in the Mozu cluster, including those that are partially destroyed. Of these, 19 have been designated as national historic sites,{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1797 |title=百舌鳥古墳群|trans-title=Mozu kofungun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}} and separately, the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] has ruled three to be Imperial mausoleums, two to be "Tomb Reference Sites", and 18 to be "baichō", or ancillary mausoleums connected with an Imperial mausoleum.Of the Kofuns, the Itasuke Kofun was designated as a national historic site in 1956, and 19 units have been designated by 2019, including those that were additionally designated after that (Notice of the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology on October 15, 2018). No. 193, February 26, 2019, Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 25). There used to be more than 100 Kofuns, but due to the rapid development of residential land after World War II, more than half of the Kofuns were destroyed.[http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/database/index.html 古墳データベース] – [http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/index.html 堺市ウェブサイト]{{smaller|（2017th year 6月11日閲覧）}} In 2010, the Japanese government proposed that the Daisen Kofun and the entire cluster of Mozu Tombs and [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Tombs]] be designated as a [[:en:UNESCO World Heritage Site|UNESCO World Heritage Site]].{{cite web|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/tentativelists/5570/|title=Mozu-Furuichi Kofungun, Ancient Kofun Clusters – UNESCO World Heritage Centre|first=UNESCO World Heritage|last=Centre|work=unesco.org|access-date=23 February 2017}} 9 years later on 6 July 2019, the site was approved and inscribed as a UNESCO [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]] under Criteria: (iii) and (iv) as the Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Group: Mounded Tombs of Ancient Japan.{{cite web|url=https://en.unesco.org/news/seven-cultural-sites-inscribed-unescos-world-heritage-list|title=Seven cultural sites inscribed on UNESCO's World Heritage List|website=UNESCO|date=6 July 2019}} ==Features== The ''kofun'' are found in many shapes and dimensions in varying patterns. Some are of simple [[Round Kofun|circular]] or [[Square Kofun|square shape]] (''empun'' and ''hōfun''). The larger ones are keyhole-shaped (''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|zempō kōenfun]]''); they represent the highest class of ''kofun'' and were built in great detail. The three prominent aspects of these ''kofun'' are their massive size and being surrounded by several moats and many secondary ''kofun''. In the [[:en:Osaka Plain|Osaka Plain]] and [[:en:Nara Basin|Nara Basin]], which were the cultural centre of the Kofun Period, the rounded keyhole-shaped tombs were built extending to very large lengths, out of which the Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Clusters are the most prominent. These are in two ''kofun'' groups which are dated to the later part of 4th and early part of 6th centuries. These ''kofun'' are of the largest dimensions in the country. The Nintoku-tennō-ryō Kofun, is one grave mound which is a {{Convert|486|m}} long tumulus enclosed by a moat and a [[:en:Rampart (fortification)|fortification]] which is {{Convert|840|m}} in length; this is said to be the largest such mound in the world. This cluster also has the Richū-tennō-ryō Kofun, made of a tumulus of {{Convert|360|m}} length and said to be the third largest in the country. Another group of mounds, located about {{Convert|10|km}} away from the Mozu cluster is known as the Furuichi cluster. It has the Ōjin-tennō-ryō Kofun of {{Convert|425|m}} length which is said to be the second largest in the country. This group also has 11 more huge massive rounded "keyhole-shaped kofun" with mound length of {{Convert|200|m}} or more. A feature of these funerary mounds is that they contain – along with the buried people – [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] made of iron, weapons worn by individuals including arrowheads, swords, hoe and spade tips, and many other similar items. Also found in the mounds are antiquities made of gilded bronze such as horse tacks and sash buckles. The Daisen Kofun mound is approximately {{convert|500|m}} long and {{convert|300|m}} across at its widest point, while the entire tomb area is {{convert|840|m}} long.{{cite web|url=http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/database/nintokutenno.html|title=仁徳天皇陵古墳（大山古墳） 堺市|first=Sakai|last=city|work=sakai.lg.jp|access-date=23 February 2017}} Enclosed by three moats, the mound rises approximately 35m above the surrounding terrain. The highest point is {{convert|47|m}}, making it visible to the seafarers in nearby Osaka Bay.{{Cite web |title=Mozu Daisenryo Kofun – Japan Airlines |url=https://jal.japantravel.com/osaka/mozu-daisenryo-kofun/9100 |access-date=2022-03-20 |website=jal.japantravel.com}} The inner moat is the widest of the moats at approximately {{convert|60|m}}. The mound is approximately {{convert|100000|sqm}} in area, and the entire tomb is {{convert|460000|sqm}}. Today, the tomb is off-limits and protected by the Imperial Household Agency in the centre of Sakai City. The moats have been maintained and provide a sanctuary for fish and waterbirds. The mound itself is completely overgrown by vegetation. A viewing platform from the second (middle) moat is accessible at the south side of the site. The viewing platform is 500m away from [[:en:Mozu Station|Mozu Station]] on the [[:en:Hanwa Line|Hanwa Line]] and is directly across the street from the [[:en:Sakai City Museum|Sakai City Museum]].{{cite web|url=http://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/hakubutsukan/index.html|title=堺市博物館 堺市|first=Sakai|last=city|work=sakai.lg.jp|access-date=23 February 2017}} This museum provides visitors with information about the ''kofun'' and its history. === Gallery === File:NintokuTomb Aerial photograph 2007.jpg|alt=|Daisenryo Kofun, 2.8 km in circumference and the largest kofun in [[:en:Japan|Japan]], is thought to be the Tomb of [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]]. File:Kamiishizu Misanzai Kofun Aerial photograph 2007.jpg|alt=|Kamiishizu Misanzai Kofun File:Haze Nisanzai Kofun zenkei.JPG|Haze Nisanzai Kofun File:Mozu Gobyo-yama Kofun zenkei.JPG|Mozu Gobyo-yama Kofun File:Tadeiyama Kofun Aerial photograph 2007.jpg|alt=|Tadeiyama Kofun File:Itasuke Kofun zenkei.JPG|Itasuke Kofun {| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;white-space:nowrap;font-size:85%"
